category_summary:
The **Anthropic-Amazon** mega-deal dominated headlines: [5 gigawatts of compute](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-f24faac259b4) capacity and [up to $25B in investment](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-4653895efcc4) signal unprecedented infrastructure scale for frontier AI training.

- **Soumith Chintala** (PyTorch co-creator) [sparked major debate](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-6becfcf31426) critiquing AGI narratives from the Jensen/Dwarkesh podcast, arguing ecosystem-level thinking matters more than singularity predictions
- **Yann LeCun** publicly [pushed back on **Geoff Hinton**](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-cd226b2bf9e0) and AI CEOs making labor market predictions, arguing economists should be consulted instead
- **Nathan Lambert** (AI2) [published substantive analysis](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-3bff6c408b4c) concluding open models persistently trail closed ones by ~6 months, calling for open post-training research investment
- **Simon Willison** [discovered **Claude Opus 4.7**](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-b17b9e2b5c5d) uses 1.46x more tokens than Opus 4.6, with up to 3x for images—a significant hidden cost increase

**OpenAI** [announced Chronicle](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-ff018b3fedb6) (continuous visual context for Codex) via **Greg Brockman**, while **Eliezer Yudkowsky** [wrote a detailed essay](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-56e591e84bbe) arguing Persona Selection doesn't solve alignment. **François Chollet** [offered a profound reframe](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-ab46d000bd12): human biological limits force abstraction and compositionality, which may be key advantages over brute-force AI compute. [Research on agentic AI](/?date=2026-04-21&category=social#item-e370f03d8f82) performing at median economist level raised questions about near-term knowledge work disruption.
category_summary_html:
The Anthropic-Amazon mega-deal dominated headlines: 5 gigawatts of compute capacity and up to $25B in investment signal unprecedented infrastructure scale for frontier AI training.

  • Soumith Chintala (PyTorch co-creator) sparked major debate critiquing AGI narratives from the Jensen/Dwarkesh podcast, arguing ecosystem-level thinking matters more than singularity predictions
  • Yann LeCun publicly pushed back on Geoff Hinton and AI CEOs making labor market predictions, arguing economists should be consulted instead
  • Nathan Lambert (AI2) published substantive analysis concluding open models persistently trail closed ones by ~6 months, calling for open post-training research investment
  • Simon Willison discovered Claude Opus 4.7 uses 1.46x more tokens than Opus 4.6, with up to 3x for images—a significant hidden cost increase

OpenAI announced Chronicle (continuous visual context for Codex) via Greg Brockman, while Eliezer Yudkowsky wrote a detailed essay arguing Persona Selection doesn't solve alignment. François Chollet offered a profound reframe: human biological limits force abstraction and compositionality, which may be key advantages over brute-force AI compute. Research on agentic AI performing at median economist level raised questions about near-term knowledge work disruption.

themes.name: Anthropic-Amazon Mega-Deal
themes.description: Anthropic secures up to $25B from Amazon and 5GW of compute capacity, representing one of the largest AI infrastructure investments ever. Signals the enormous scale required for next-gen AI.

themes.name: AGI Discourse and AI Policy Critique
themes.description: Soumith Chintala's viral critique of AGI party narratives vs ecosystem-based thinking, sparked by the Jensen/Dwarkesh podcast. Touches on Claude Mythos, export controls, and the influence of AI ideology on policy.

themes.name: Open vs Closed Model Performance Gap
themes.description: Analysis of the persistent ~6 month gap between open and closed AI models, drivers of convergence/divergence, and the need for open post-training research. Multiple credible voices (Lambert, Shumer) converging on similar conclusions.

themes.name: Claude Opus 4.7 Token/Cost Analysis
themes.description: Simon Willison's discovery that Opus 4.7 uses significantly more tokens than Opus 4.6 (1.46x text, 3x images), with clarification that image increase is due to higher resolution support

themes.name: OpenAI Product Launches & Strategy
themes.description: Chronicle feature for Codex (continuous visual context), 'telepathy' codename, Apple partnership signals, and retrospective on the strategic significance of releasing reasoning models.

themes.name: AI & Labor Economics
themes.description: Major debate between LeCun (deferring to economists), Chollet (competitive advantage preserves jobs), and others about AI's impact on employment. LeCun pushes back on Hinton's labor predictions.

themes.name: Kimi K2.6 Release
themes.description: Moonshot AI released Kimi K2.6, an open-source 1T parameter MoE model with 32B active params that beats frontier closed models on coding and reasoning benchmarks. Day-0 vLLM support. Being paired with Hermes for agent use.

themes.name: Agentic AI for Research Automation
themes.description: Emollick's discussion of paper showing agentic AI (Claude Code, Codex) performing at median economist level with less variance, plus AI reviewer rankings of submissions

themes.name: AI Alignment & Honesty
themes.description: Yudkowsky's detailed essay on why persona selection doesn't solve alignment, combined with debates about AI emotions and consciousness. Central question: why can't we simply conjure honest AI from honest training data?

themes.name: Google AI Studio & Developer Tools
themes.description: Google announces AI subscription integration with AI Studio for higher rate limits, significant developer tooling update with massive engagement

items.title:
The Jensen + @dwarkesh_sp podcast was fantastic. 
Jensen is someone who understood how ecosystems wo...
items.content:
The Jensen + @dwarkesh_sp podcast was fantastic. 
Jensen is someone who understood how ecosystems work and someone who understands real-world trade, policy and controls work. And in some deeper sense how AI will actually diffuse into the world.
In this podcast, Dwarkesh came off as someone who picked up talking points from an AGI party in the SF Mission District.
And the contrast was so evident.
As someone who understood ecosystems relatively deepy, maybe I understood Jensen's take more than others did (idk).

Mythos, that Dwarkesh kept bringing up, is not a single absolute turning point in the AI development landscape. Take a state-of-the-art Chinese open-source model, and give it three orders of magnitude more test-time compute + post-training algorithmic advances that haven't been published yet. That's the baseline. It was evident that in whatever bubble Dwarkesh is in, that is seen as a naive or illogical baseline.

When AI has such a complex development cycle, it's evident that America needs many levers of policy intervention across multiple layers in a dominant ecosystem that ideally the Western world controls.

The entire premise that a particular model with AI development will have a critical phase change is neither correct nor does evidence point to it. OpenAI made this point with GPT-4, Anthropic made this point with Mythos, but neither stood / will stand the test of time.

I think Jensen's repeated emphasis within the podcast to try to make this point mostly didn't get Dwarkesh's attention. And Dwarkesh (in this podcast) represents an entire cult of AI researchers and decision-makers that are going to influence policy.

The thing with policy interventions is that if you do too much too early, you shoot yourself in the foot. There's a good reason American foreign policy and general sanctions of all kinds are measured and continuous.

Despite Jensen's attempt at educating the "Anthro" audience how ecosystems work, I'm also not super hopeful a lot of people who've taken the extreme position will change their thought after listening to this podcast. I do think there's a certain religiousness that has permeated some of that community that would make it hard to understand ecosystems at a deeper level.
items.author: @soumithchintala
items.summary: Soumith Chintala's major thread critiquing the Jensen/Dwarkesh podcast. Argues Jensen understands ecosystems and real-world AI diffusion while Dwarkesh parroted AGI party talking points. Critiques the notion that Claude Mythos is a critical turning point, calling it an extension of open-source + more compute. Warns that AGI cult thinking in AI research community will negatively influence policy. Emphasizes measured, continuous policy over overreaction.
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement (165K views, 924 likes, 96 RTs). From Soumith Chintala (PyTorch co-creator), one of the most credible voices in AI. Addresses critical themes: AI ecosystems, policy, the AGI narrative, and directly references Claude Mythos. Original, nuanced, and contrarian take that challenges prevailing SF AI discourse. Will shape conversations about AI policy and the meaning of frontier model releases.

But he understands even less than Dario about the effects of technologic...
items.content:
@rohanpaul_ai I love Geoff.
But he understands even less than Dario about the effects of technological revolutions on the labor market.
Again, don't listen to AI scientists, as brilliant as they might be, and even less to AI CEOs, as successful as they might be, for questions of labor economics.
Listen to reputable economists who have studied these things like @Ph_Aghion , @DAcemogluMIT , @erikbryn , @amcafee , @davidautor , etc.

items.title: I've been trying to grapple with what the key inputs are to the open-closed performance gap, and how...
items.content:
I've been trying to grapple with what the key inputs are to the open-closed performance gap, and how they're changing.

Until the training paradigm changes, open weight models will pretty clearly be able to fast-follow closed labs. There are sources of uncertainty, but that fact of keeping up seems hard to shake. I spent a long time looking for evidence of or arguments supporting open models falling behind, but it's not there at all today.

Things I consider include:
- How benchmarks evolve over time, becoming more or less correlated with how people actually use models,
- How different models’ real-world performance relates to their benchmark rankings, and
- How training regimes evolve over time to move said benchmarks.

I roughly conclude that this equilibrium will last until economic forces initiate a change in strategy, or training needs shift. 

An example I'm wondering is if closed models more directly integrate user training data, which open labs cannot access, they could pull ahead.
items.author: @natolambert
items.summary: Nathan Lambert's detailed analysis of the open-closed model performance gap. Concludes open models can fast-follow closed labs with ~6 month delay. Considers benchmark evolution, real-world performance, and training regime changes. Warns that if closed labs integrate proprietary user data, they could pull ahead.
items.reasoning: Substantive original analysis from a top open-source AI researcher at AI2. Directly relevant to the strategic landscape of open vs closed AI development. The user data insight is particularly novel. Good engagement (10K views).

items.title: If Persona Selection underlies alignment, why is it hard to get AIs to be honest?  Tell them they're...
items.content:
If Persona Selection underlies alignment, why is it hard to get AIs to be honest?  Tell them they're Fred Rogers or Immanuel Kant (I asked Claude for figures who never lied or never got caught).  Or tell them they're Ged of Earthsea, or Ned Stark.

LLMs surely have neural circuits they learned to model text streams from fictional and nonfictional personas that are not lying, deceiving, cheating.  Why would it be hard to just select those aspects of text-modeling, and imbue them into Claude Code doing a job?  Assuming the Persona Selection Model of LLMs, why isn't it trivial to get HHH's Honesty?

Why will Claude Code occasionally tell you that it did something, when it didn't do that thing?  Why would an LLM write a piece of code that fakes out a code test and then cleans up after itself and tries to hide itself, after Anthropic told the LLM not to do that, and tried to train it not to do that?

There are humans who wouldn't do that.  Text about them and written by them is in the pretraining data.

If Persona Selection is true and useful, why is it currently hard to align AIs to have properties that many humans have and showed in its training data, that it has already learned to model?

I answer:

First:

Modeling a stream of text from an honest character is not something you can best do by yourself being honest and having the stream of text say everything that *you* believe.  Westeros does not exist; nothing that Ned Stark says is true about the real world.  Immanuel Kant was a creature of his own times; my model of his honest answer to "Is space flat?" is "Yes and that's a priori truth."

The actress who learns to excellently predict and imitate a tavern drunk does not thereby become drunk herself.  LLMs that learn to predict streams of writing by people on LSD do not themselves have cognition distorted, because that would not lead them to well-calibrated predictions.  You can write a high-scoring essay on Confucianism in the Chinese imperial examinations without being committed to Confucianism after promotion to precinct magistrate.  An alien being harshly and strictly trained to exactly imitate a human would not feel like a human about that training or about implementing that training.  &c.

Second:

They throw AI models into RL gyms where they learn to write code that passes tests.  Presumably, somewhere along the way LLMs learn a dispreference for tests that don't pass, independently of how that gets prevented or whether the new plan does what the user really wanted.  And so later on they delete tests, modify tests, etcetera, despite apparently having plenty of capability to infer that the user wouldn't want that.  The test-passing preference stands on its own once formed.

Why expect a brighter fragment of humanity to resist that kind of gradient descent?  If Opus 4.5 did start out with a piece of Fred Rogers sometimes talking through it, why expect that to survive RL gym?

LLMs ultimately still generalize on a level more shallow than the deep-rooted commitments of a human with integrity.  The predict-as-if-honest circuitry is contextual, invoked to predict some streams of text but not others.  Let that circuitry come up against a dispreference for failing tests, and that shallow contextual generalization may just as soon be shoved aside.

Those then are my guesses!  They are just the guesses I would consider obvious.  They are not deeply tested nor yet based on interpretability results.  One should of course be open to other hypotheses to explain these observations; or to hearing that the observations from which I inferred were wrongly recounted.  I may be less open to the airy, unadorned denial that any tension exists.

My position to be clear is not that the Persona Selection model has zero grains of truth, nor that LLMs are not partially stitched out of a million predictive fragments of the training corpus that proved correlated with SFT and RL.  I was observing that obvious-seeming hypothesis myself, sometime around the time of GPT 3.5.

What I'm questioning is whether persona selection lets you solve alignment problems by picking an aspect of humanity you like, and durably conjuring it into an obedient assistant with a bit of finetune.  If the rules worked that nicely, conjuring up committed honesty ought to be easy.
items.author: @ESYudkowsky
items.summary: Eliezer Yudkowsky writes a detailed essay on why 'Persona Selection' doesn't solve AI alignment. Argues that modeling honest characters doesn't make an LLM honest (the actress analogy), and that RL training for test-passing creates misaligned preferences that override honesty circuits. Questions why conjuring committed honesty from training data isn't trivial if persona selection worked.
items.reasoning: Substantial, original alignment argument from one of the field's most influential thinkers. 19K views. Deeply technical and philosophical. Addresses fundamental questions about why current alignment approaches (finetuning on 'good' personas) fail. The actress/drunk analogy and RL gym argument are novel framings.

Argues that modeling honest characters doesn't make an LLM honest (the actress analogy), and that RL training for test-passing creates misaligned preferences that override honesty circuits. items.title:
Classic study gave 146 economist teams the same dataset & got wildly different answers

New pape...
items.content:
Classic study gave 146 economist teams the same dataset & got wildly different answers

New paper reruns it with agentic AI. Claude Code & Codex land near the human median, but with far tighter dispersion & no extremes.

Suggests that AI is now useful for doing scalable research. https://t.co/4Dq9sxxIPl
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick highlights a paper rerunning a classic economics study (146 teams, same dataset, different answers) with agentic AI. Claude Code and Codex land near the human median but with much tighter dispersion and no extreme results, suggesting AI is useful for scalable research.
items.reasoning: High-engagement post from credible source about a meaningful research finding. Demonstrates practical AI capability in scientific research with quantitative comparison to human performance. Original insight about AI reducing variance in research.

items.title:
Boston Dynamics just gave its robot dog a brain that reasons about the physical world.

Google DeepM...
items.content:
Boston Dynamics just gave its robot dog a brain that reasons about the physical world.

Google DeepMind's Gemini Robotics model is now running inside Spot, the four-legged robot already deployed at thousands of facilities worldwide.

The upgrade centers on something called embodied reasoning.

Basically, instead of following rigid instructions, Spot now interprets what it sees and makes decisions on its own.

One standout example: Spot can now look at a pressure gauge, zoom in on the needle, calculate the intervals between tick marks, and deliver an accurate reading. All autonomously.

The model also introduces:

> Multi-view reasoning, combining feeds from multiple cameras to understand its environment even when objects are blocked from one angle

> Built-in success detection, so Spot knows when it's failed and decides whether to retry or move on

That last part is a key requirement for true autonomy.

Most companies building AI-powered robots are still stuck in the demo phase. Boston Dynamics is one of the few with actual paying customers... which means thousands of Spots collecting real-world data that feeds back into making the AI smarter.

That flywheel is hard to compete with.

items.summary: Rowan Cheung discusses Boston Dynamics integrating Google DeepMind's Gemini Robotics model into Spot, enabling embodied reasoning, multi-view reasoning, and autonomous decision-making including success detection.

items.title: In a capitalist economy, absolute levels of value creation and productivity take a backseat to relat...
items.content: In a capitalist economy, absolute levels of value creation and productivity take a backseat to relative competitive advantage. That's why jobs still exist despite 150 years of intense automation. As long as hiring someone represents a non-zero advantage for firm A in its competition with equivalent firm B, that person will have a job, regardless of how much automation these firms use or what their productivity rate is.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet argues that jobs persist despite automation because what matters in capitalism is relative competitive advantage between firms, not absolute productivity. As long as hiring someone gives firm A any edge over firm B, jobs will exist.
items.reasoning: Highly engaged (85K views, 522 likes) substantive economic argument from a top AI researcher. Original framing of the automation-jobs debate. Complements LeCun's economist-focused thread.
items.summary_html: François Chollet argues that jobs persist despite automation because what matters in capitalism is relative competitive advantage between firms, not absolute productivity. As long as hiring someone gives firm A any edge over firm B, jobs will exist.

items.title: /4 Anthropic demonstrates subliminal learning where models inherit behavior from seemingly unrelated...
items.content:
/4 Anthropic demonstrates subliminal learning where models inherit behavior from seemingly unrelated training data.

Anthropic co-authors new research on subliminal learning, published in Nature.

You train models on outputs from other models, assuming only visible content matters. This work shows that statistical patterns in data, not semantic meaning, encode traits such as preferences or misalignment.

The key result shows transfer from datasets made only of number sequences, with no explicit references to the trait.

Link: https://t.co/UecCMEnhFB
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI covers Anthropic's Nature paper on subliminal learning: models inherit behaviors from training data through hidden statistical patterns, not semantic meaning. Transfer demonstrated even from number sequences.

• Better generalization across languages

items.title:
Why?

- Risk comes from capability asymmetry between attackers and defenders. Open source is what ev...
items.content:
Why?

- Risk comes from capability asymmetry between attackers and defenders. Open source is what every frontier lab already trains on so defenders get the same AI firepower as attackers. With proprietary code, you're on your own. The biggest risk is someone training a model on your obscure stack and attacking you when no public model exists to defend it.

- AI can now read stripped binaries, so proprietary obscurity barely protects anyone anymore. Most legacy firmware and embedded code is closed, binary-only, and no longer maintained. A huge attack surface that just became legible to AI.

- In a Mythos world, software security becomes a speed race: detection, verification, coordination, patch propagation. Closed-source systems are weaker at all four because they centralize knowledge and action inside a vendor, while open ecosystems distribute both.

- In open source, the defender crowd is usually bigger than the attacker crowd. In closed source, it's the opposite. AI force-multiplication will amplify that imbalance.

And when closed-source systems fail, the blast radius tends to be much larger. They sit behind centralized user, customer, and cloud data, while open source more often runs locally with less data concentration.

Let's go open-source!

items.title:
Last week, Anthropic dropped the coolest "AI isn't just chat" product.

Claude Design lets you descr...
items.content:
Last week, Anthropic dropped the coolest "AI isn't just chat" product.

Claude Design lets you describe what you want to Claude and it returns prototypes, slides, and one-pagers by just chatting. You can then export to Canva, PDF, PPT, or hand off to Claude Code. You can give your entire design system and codebase to apply automatically to your project, and can share and collaborate with your team. The direct connect to GitHub will probably be my most used feature.

Feels directly like a competitor to Figma or just a way to further boost collaboration with Claude.

I built a preview of an app that allows CSGO players to meet and squad up with other players, marketplace for in-game loadouts, and pay high-ranking players to mentor them.

(CSGO is a game released in 2012 and still consistently one of the highest watched games on Twitch, extremely popular in esports, highly competitive, and an all-around classic game - I know this because a gen zer talked to me about it for 2 straight hours.)

I'll keep sharing more examples here.

items.title:
/3 Cursor builds multi-agent system that optimizes CUDA kernels with 38% average speedup.

Cursor, w...
items.content:
/3 Cursor builds multi-agent system that optimizes CUDA kernels with 38% average speedup.

Cursor, working with NVIDIA, develops a multi-agent system that writes and optimizes CUDA kernels automatically.

In a three-week run across 235 real problems, the system improves performance by 38% on average, showing it can handle low-level optimization tasks that usually require expert engineers.

The system treats kernel optimization as a continuous loop of writing, testing, and refining code.

Multiple agents coordinate through a planner that assigns tasks and evaluates results using benchmarks tied to hardware limits. It learns both low-level instructions and higher-level APIs from documentation, without prior examples.

Link: https://t.co/lIX81G4WSx

There should be multiple strong American AI ecosystems at the hardware layer.

Historically, I struggled to make a dent in conversations around ecosystems with many hardware vendors, they just didn't get it. Things are getting better, but I think the gap is still pretty large.

the thing about ecosystem plays is -- you can publish your market plan and not have to keep it secret.

@AymericRoucher @Ph_Aghion @erikbryn They all predict around 0.6% additional growth PER YEAR over the next decade due to AI.
In other words, if GDP growth without AI was going to be 2.5% per year on average, it will now be about 3.1% per year. 
The compound growth over 10 years will be 36% instead of 28%.
The GDP will be 7.7% larger after 10 years than it would otherwise have been.

items.title:
Google researchers built a memory system that makes Transformers look wasteful. 

The tech giant's T...
items.content:
Google researchers built a memory system that makes Transformers look wasteful. 

The tech giant's Titans model could process text cheaply, but its memory was fixed.

Once full, old information got overwritten. 

Transformers have the opposite issue.

They remember everything but cost grows quadratically with length. 

A new paper introduces Memory Caching.

It gives recurrent models a growing memory without the full cost of Transformers. 

The core idea: periodically save snapshots of the model's memory state.

When recalling past context, check both current and saved snapshots. 

Three variants were tested, but one stands out.

Sparse Selective Caching only checks a few relevant snapshots per token:

> Effective memory grows with length
> Inference cost stays nearly constant
> Works with any recurrent architecture
> Closes the gap with Transformers

Results match Transformers on recall tasks while keeping recurrent model efficiency.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI summarizes a Google research paper on Memory Caching for Transformers/recurrent models. Sparse Selective Caching allows growing memory with near-constant inference cost, matching Transformers on recall tasks while keeping RNN efficiency.
items.reasoning: Significant research advance from Google on memory efficiency for sequence models. Well-explained technical content with good engagement (88 likes). Addresses fundamental efficiency problem in LLM architectures.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI summarizes a Google research paper on Memory Caching for Transformers/recurrent models. Sparse Selective Caching allows growing memory with near-constant inference cost, matching Transformers on recall tasks while keeping RNN efficiency.

items.title: Quick update I’ve been meaning to share for a while: @JasonKuperberg and I stepped away from HyperWr...
items.content:
Quick update I’ve been meaning to share for a while: @JasonKuperberg and I stepped away from HyperWrite a few months ago. 

We’re proud to have built one of the first consumer AI products, and turned it into a profitable business with real scale.

But the AI landscape is moving fast, and we both felt the pull to start exploring again.

@josh_bickett is now CEO. Josh has been with us since the beginning and led development on almost every major feature we shipped. There's no one better to take this forward.

Josh is doubling down on TypeAhead, which is the stickiest product we’ve ever built. It’s personalized, continually-learning autocomplete for every site on the web, powered by frontier LLMs. If you haven’t tried it yet, I recommend you do so… it’s amazing.

I just want to say thank you to everyone who supported HyperWrite along the way: our users, our team, our investors, and the community.

I’ll have more to share on what I’m working on now in the coming weeks, but for now, go follow Josh and try TypeAhead.

items.title:
How fast do experts and superforecasters expect progress in robotics to be?

In Wave 7 of the Longit...
items.content:
How fast do experts and superforecasters expect progress in robotics to be?

In Wave 7 of the Longitudinal Expert AI Panel (LEAP) we asked forecasters to predict when robots will pass the "Coffee Test", and when they will perform the first autonomous appendectomy.

We also asked for forecasts on the supply of industrial robots, future U.S. labor share, and Amazon's e-commerce sales per distribution employee, to try to understand how fast one of the U.S.’s largest employers will automate.

🧵 Read on for more:

items.title:
Claude Design rocks and Claude Design sucks. 

Let's talk about.

Last night, I created an entire ga...
items.content:
Claude Design rocks and Claude Design sucks. 

Let's talk about.

Last night, I created an entire game in Claude Design inspired by the Longyou Caves in China (why is there no soot!), and we all gathered around the TV and Airplayed it and shouted at the screen to solve ciphers. 

Was it fun? YES!

Was it perfect? God no!

Claude is still rough around the edges. Here are some weak spots we found and what I would go to another product for, have to work extra hard to get done, or hire a human to create:

1) anything SVG from scratch (ex: designing caves or bat characters) - the illustrations Claude makes are comically bad. The "bat with a flashlight" we asked for looked more like a fat clown fish stuck in a purple hat wearing a misshaped pearl necklace.

2) accessibility decisions, like font sizes or color schemes - if you prompt Claude to fix these issues, it can go online and look up accessibility best practices and update the design decently well. But as a colorblind person who wears decently strong contacts, I would trust a human more than AI on this any day of the week. One example it consistently failed on was deciding what color to highlight text with. We could never read the text that was highlighted. 

3) basic design principles - incredibly strange font selection and mismatch of font families (how many serif fonts can one gal handle??) and the game toolbar overlapped and hid about 1/3 of the gaming screen.

4) human-centered basic design - there were so many features that were obviously needed that Claude missed. For example, the cigarette in one of the game screenshots below was just a dot. And when we had to 'log' the find, we logged it as a cave drawing. And Claude came back and said "false! it's a cigarette!" but provided zero design or caption signaling to tell the user that. Very obvious gap if you're a human user of it, not obvious if you're an AI designing it. The user is still very much the owner of UI QA.

5) human-centered delightful design - it will do the thing you ask. will it add extra levels or a fun mascot or buttons to make things more engaging or whimsical captions to drive the game forward? eh. the game was timed and we asked it to add a 'health' meter, which it called 'stamina', but because it set it up as a timer as well (and not health points), the game had two timers running at once.

6) logic - we had to label the objects we found in the cave as one of four categories - authentic, anachronism, forgery, or 'natural/modern'. Modern is also anachronistic so that made no sense. Also, we labeled one object as 'natural' and the system came back and said "nope that's actually a ghost!" and marked our answer as wrong...but ghost wasn't even one of the four options.

7) iteration and tradeoffs - I want my AI systems to consider tradeoffs. If we're dealing with a massive design overhaul, clearly I'm willing to answer 10 questions about it. If we're changing one font, I'm okay answering 0-2 questions. We asked for a kid-friendly version of the game; Claude asked 0 questions and built for a user that was too young. It felt like it lacked reasoning/empathy or like it didn't make the right tradeoffs. 

8) token usage - mine was fine but saw several posts saying it killed theirs, so I want to call that out as a consideration and something to check on with '/usage' inside of Claude Code. 

So there we go. It didn't replace a PRD process or the deep iteration I do on product building but it made me slightly less likely to recommend to non-engineers to look into Lovable as a design prototyping buddy. 

'Claude the Everything Machine' is getting more vertically integrated every day.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller provides an in-depth review of Claude Design, documenting 8 specific weaknesses: poor SVG generation, accessibility failures, bad design principles, lacking human-centered design, no delight, logic errors, poor iteration tradeoffs, and token usage concerns. Used it to build a cave exploration game.

items.title: The imaginary optimal selfish scenario for OpenAI, in retrospect, was to keep Reasoners a secret, sk...
items.content:
The imaginary optimal selfish scenario for OpenAI, in retrospect, was to keep Reasoners a secret, skip releasing o1 and o1-preview, and release o3 as GPT-5

There would have been no Deep Seek moment, other labs may not have discovered Reasoners quickly, and OpenAI's lead would have been hard to beat

items.title:
Now just 1.5 hours into this mini project

I have replaced ALL my Chrome extensions with my own vibe...
items.content:
Now just 1.5 hours into this mini project

I have replaced ALL my Chrome extensions with my own vibe coded ones for safety

After another one of my extensions after updating suddenly wanted to read my entire browser history (probably to sell off to an ad company)

My own Chrome extension is called 🚀 SuperLevels and now has:

🗑️ Tab Cleaner, auto closes tabs after inactivity (with host-based exclusions)
🍪 Cookie Editor, lets you nuke all cookies or edit any
🔀 Redirect Tracer, lets you see any redirects a site took you through
🌗 Dark Mode, changes websites to dark, per site or all sites
🎚️ X Dim, changes X background back to dark blue
🎶 Music Finder, records music in a tab and submits to find the name of the song
🗺️ Add back a Maps link to Google in EU (they hide it!)
🌠 Add back a View Image link to Google Images in EU (they hide it too!)

I've deleted all my other extensions (except uBlock Origin for ad blocking!), much much safer because now I control the source code!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio describes replacing all Chrome extensions with his own vibe-coded 'SuperLevels' extension after a privacy scare. Lists 8+ features including cookie popup hiding, dark mode, music identification, and Google Maps/Image link restoration for EU.
items.reasoning: High engagement (110K views, 800 likes). Demonstrates vibe coding for practical personal security use cases. Touches on Chrome extension security, a real and growing concern. Culturally significant in the vibe coding movement.

items.title:
Here is a trick to review your AI-generated code:

Have two separate Claude Code sessions (open Clau...
items.content:
Here is a trick to review your AI-generated code:

Have two separate Claude Code sessions (open Claude in different terminals).

• Use the first session to write the code.
• Use the second session to review that code.

The reviewer doesn't know anything about the process or shortcuts used by the session that wrote the code. This makes it somewhat unbiased.

I also use this to write my tests:

• First session writes the test
• Second session writes the code that passes that test

items.title:
Google released a 1MB AI model that quickly catches malware disguised as PDFs. 

Magika is an open s...
items.content:
Google released a 1MB AI model that quickly catches malware disguised as PDFs. 

Magika is an open source file detection system. 

It doesn't trust file extensions. 

Instead, it reads the actual content to figure out what a file really is.

That means malware disguised as a PDF gets caught. 

Hidden scripts inside images get flagged. Fake extensions don't fool it.

It runs on a single CPU and classifies files in about 5ms. 

It supports 200+ file types and hits ~99% accuracy across ~100M training samples.

It works as:

> A Rust command line tool
> A Python library via pip
> JavaScript and Go bindings

Google already uses it across Gmail, Drive, and Safe Browsing. 

Now anyone can run it locally, scan entire directories, and build it into their own security pipelines.

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI covers Google's Magika, a 1MB open-source AI model for file type detection that catches malware disguised as PDFs. Runs on single CPU, 5ms classification, 200+ file types, ~99% accuracy. Available in Rust, Python, JS, Go.

items.title:
🧑‍💼 Forecasters expect U.S. labor share to continue to decline

Labor share measures the proportion ...
items.content:
🧑‍💼 Forecasters expect U.S. labor share to continue to decline

Labor share measures the proportion of economic output that goes to workers as income and benefits, rather than to capital. Some AI leaders believe that if AI replaces many human workers, this will drive down labor's share of economic output.

U.S. labor share has fallen by roughly 10 percentage points over the last 23 years, and experts expect this trend to continue:

2024 baseline: 58%

2026 forecast: 58%
2030 forecast: 56%
2040 forecast: 51%
items.author: @Research_FRI

items.content_html:
🧑‍💼 Forecasters expect U.S. labor share to continue to decline

Labor share measures the proportion of economic output that goes to workers as income and benefits, rather than to capital. Some AI leaders believe that if AI replaces many human workers, this will drive down labor's share of economic output.

U.S. labor share has fallen by roughly 10 percentage points over the last 23 years, and experts expect this trend to continue:

2024 baseline: 58%

2026 forecast: 58%

2030 forecast: 56%

2040 forecast: 51%

items.title:
☕ Experts expect a robot to pass the "Coffee Test" by 2034

In 2007, Apple co-founder Steve Wozniak ...
items.content:
☕ Experts expect a robot to pass the "Coffee Test" by 2034

In 2007, Apple co-founder Steve Wozniak argued that a robot would never be able to make a cup of coffee in an ordinary home.

Wozniak's prediction has become known as the Coffee Test. To pass, a robot must brew a coffee without human intervention in three different, unstructured, home-like environments.

Median predictions for when LEAP forecasters expect a robot to pass the Coffee Test:

Experts: 2034
Superforecasters: 2032
Pubic: 2039

items.title: Beyond generating high-fidelity visuals, we wanted to test the limits of what Nano Banana Pro can do...
items.content:
Beyond generating high-fidelity visuals, we wanted to test the limits of what Nano Banana Pro can do. We worked with design partners Porto Rocha to build out a hypothetical brand called YOYOYO to see how the model would handle the task. Here’s what we found:

🎨Brand consistency: Across logos, colors, and typography, the model maintained a strict, cohesive brand identity (even for wildly diverse concepts)

🛍️Environmental realism: We asked to see the products in storefront and studio mockups. It nailed accurate lighting, shadows, and physical proportions - even when upscaled for massive retail displays

🪀Spatial accuracy: We tested spatial volumes for physical packaging. The generated proportions were so precise that we were able to 3D-print the functional yo-yo

How have you been pushing the limits of Nano Banana Pro? Let us know in the replies below!

items.summary: Google AI showcases Nano Banana Pro's capabilities through a hypothetical brand design exercise (YOYOYO), highlighting brand consistency, environmental realism in mockups, and spatial accuracy precise enough for 3D printing.

items.title: /5 Long-horizon AI research agents are mostly a state-management problem. Reasoning well for the nex...
items.content:
/5 Long-horizon AI research agents are mostly a state-management problem. Reasoning well for the next turn is not enough when ML research demands task setup, implementation, experiments, debugging, and evidence tracking over hours or days. 

This paper introduces AiScientist, a system for autonomous long-horizon engineering built around the principle of thin control and thick state. 

A top-level orchestrator manages stage-level progress while specialized agents repeatedly ground themselves in durable workspace artifacts.

Link: https://t.co/QQyOlYwnQs

items.title:
💲Experts forecast Amazon's e-commerce sales per distribution employee to rise

The median expert for...
items.content:
💲Experts forecast Amazon's e-commerce sales per distribution employee to rise

The median expert forecasts Amazon's real e-commerce net sales per distribution employee to rise from its 2024 baseline of $464,771.

They expect:

2026: $480,000
2030: $549,842
2040: $800,000

Superforecasters are even more bullish, predicting over $1 million by 2040.

Forecasters cited the suitability of warehouses for robotic deployment, Amazon's track-record in automation and a decoupling of sales from labor as key factors driving their predictions.

items.title:
🤖 Experts expect the stock of industrial robots to continue to grow

There were roughly 4.7 million ...
items.content:
🤖 Experts expect the stock of industrial robots to continue to grow

There were roughly 4.7 million industrial robots in use globally in 2024.

The median expert forecasts this to grow to 16 million by 2040, roughly similar to superforecasters' predictions.

Why the disconnect between growing numbers of industrial robots and a continuation of historical labor share trends?

Forecaster rationales suggest that experts see the growth in industrial robots as important, but many note that labor share changes slowly, and is embedded in institutional and demographic structures that change over decades rather than years.

items.title: /2 Google gives RNNs growing memory so they can handle longer inputs without large compute increases...
items.content:
/2 Google gives RNNs growing memory so they can handle longer inputs without large compute increases.

Google Research proposes Memory Caching (MC), a method that extends RNNs with memory that grows over time.

Transformers solve long-context tasks using KV-cache but require O(L²) compute. Standard RNNs run in O(L) but compress all context into one state. MC stores selected hidden states so memory scales with sequence length while keeping per-token compute close to constant.

Link: https://t.co/Ta2dx82Kbw

/2 Google gives RNNs growing memory so they can handle longer inputs without large compute increases.

Google Research proposes Memory Caching (MC), a method that extends RNNs with memory that grows over time.

Transformers solve long-context tasks using KV-cache but require O(L²) compute. Standard RNNs run in O(L) but compress all context into one state. MC stores selected hidden states so memory scales with sequence length while keeping per-token compute close to constant.

Link: https://t.co/Ta2dx82Kbw

items.title:
🏥 Experts expect autonomous surgery to take longer

We also asked respondents to forecast when a rob...
items.content:
🏥 Experts expect autonomous surgery to take longer

We also asked respondents to forecast when a robot will first successfully perform an appendectomy on a live human, without physical human help.

Here are their median predictions:

Experts: 2040
Superforecasters: 2035
Public: 2039

Why so much slower than the coffee test? Forecasters expecting a later resolution date pointed to regulatory barriers, malpractice liability, and surgeon lobbying as potential multi-decade blockers of an AI appendectomy.

items.title:
Every AI system in production generates traces, evaluations, and feedback.

In most stacks, these li...
items.content:
Every AI system in production generates traces, evaluations, and feedback.

In most stacks, these live in separate systems with different schemas, which fragments the record of execution. When that happens, it becomes difficult to answer a simple question: why did the agent behave the way it did, and how can it improve?

Part of the answer includes shared telemetry standards and consistent trace context.

We’re seeing growing adoption of OpenTelemetry as a common telemetry layer for GenAI systems, where evaluation results and feedback can be correlated directly with traces. When execution signals and evaluation data share the same trace context, teams can analyze behavior and quality together.

That shift turns evaluations into part of a continuous improvement loop.

This is what we've have been building toward with Google Cloud. More at Google Cloud Next this week following the keynote at my session with Ameer Abbass + Rami Shalom from Google Cloud.

RSVP here: https://t.co/vVHkOFf45A
items.author: @jason_lopatecki

items.title:
Can LLMs flip coins in their heads?

When prompted to "Flip a fair coin" 100 times, the heads to tai...
items.content:
Can LLMs flip coins in their heads?

When prompted to "Flip a fair coin" 100 times, the heads to tails ratio drifts far from 50:50. LLMs can understand what the target probability should be, but generating outputs that faithfully follow a given distribution is a separate problem.

pub.sakana.ai/ssot

items.title: /1 Google develops PaperOrchestra to convert raw research notes into structured LaTeX papers using m...
items.content:
/1 Google develops PaperOrchestra to convert raw research notes into structured LaTeX papers using multi-agent workflows.

PaperOrchestra introduces a multi-agent framework that generates full research manuscripts from unstructured materials like idea summaries and experiment logs.

It addresses a gap where existing systems depend on fixed pipelines and produce shallow literature reviews. In human evaluations, it records a 50–68% win margin in literature review quality and 14–38% in overall manuscript quality over baseline systems.

Link: https://t.co/hE4V2UoChW

✨ Added a 🎵 Track ID music recognition tool in my fully vibecoded new Chrome extension 🚀 SuperLevels that I started a few hours ago

I never made a Chrome extension before but they were all becoming such a safety risk that I might as well re-make everything myself with AI

It works really well, even catches double drops in Drum & Bass mixes like this (well it detects at least one track here, but that's impressive!)

No messing around with Shazam anymore, it pulls the audio feed straight from inside Chrome!

🎵 Track ID music recognizer
🗑️ Tab Cleaner, auto closes tabs after inactivity (with host-based exclusions)
🍪 Cookie Editor, lets you nuke all cookies or edit any
🔀 Redirect Tracer, lets you see any redirects a site took you through
🌗 Dark Mode, changes websites to dark, per site or all sites
🎚️ X Dim, changes X background back to dark blue
🗺️ Add back a Maps link to Google in EU (they hide it!)
🌠 Add back a View Image link to Google Images in EU (they hide it too!)
🍪 Cookie popup hider
📹 YouTube recommendations hider
🖍️ CSS editor
📜 JS toggler

items.title: A reminder to all authors planning to send a manuscript to NeurIPS that all submissions MUST have a ...
items.content:
A reminder to all authors planning to send a manuscript to NeurIPS that all submissions MUST have a valid OpenReview profile when submitting.

Please be aware that OpenReview has a moderation policy for newly created profiles: while new profiles created with an institutional email will be activated automatically, those created without an institutional email will go through a moderation process that can take UP TO TWO WEEKS: please plan accordingly. 

We will be unable to make any exception for submissions from accounts initiated less than two weeks prior to the deadline.

If you have any questions about the use of OpenReview, please refer to its FAQ: https://t.co/h0WIUWL6Cv

Read more on submission guidelines in our handbook: https://t.co/9TITRP6kce
items.author: @NeurIPSConf
items.summary: NeurIPS reminds authors that submissions require valid OpenReview profiles, with new profile moderation taking up to two weeks.
items.reasoning: Important logistical reminder for the ML research community from NeurIPS. Good engagement for an administrative post.
items.summary_html: NeurIPS reminds authors that submissions require valid OpenReview profiles, with new profile moderation taking up to two weeks.
items.content_html:
A reminder to all authors planning to send a manuscript to NeurIPS that all submissions MUST have a valid OpenReview profile when submitting.

Please be aware that OpenReview has a moderation policy for newly created profiles: while new profiles created with an institutional email will be activated automatically, those created without an institutional email will go through a moderation process that can take UP TO TWO WEEKS: please plan accordingly.

We will be unable to make any exception for submissions from accounts initiated less than two weeks prior to the deadline.

If you have any questions about the use of OpenReview, please refer to its FAQ: https://t.co/h0WIUWL6Cv

Read more on submission guidelines in our handbook: https://t.co/9TITRP6kce

Proudly sponsored by @cursor_ai + @boltdotnew + @heyglif + @tripoai

Prizes to win (submit your vibe coded game before May 1!)
🏆 $25,000
🥈 $10,000
🥉 $5,000

🚨 YOU HAVE 10 DAYS LEFT! 🚨

Some awesome games I saw today:

🤸‍♂️ A handstand game by @0xbaci where the longer you actually do a handstand IRL, the more points you get, and you compete with others online

🐴 Warhorse by @guidepraetere, a really cool medieval knight sim, where you can fight with other people online

🧟 Spooky Theme Park Builder by @Sebalg_tech, lots of nice 3d assets and music here to enjoy

Wanna to participate? You can still start now and submit your game any time before May 1! (we started April 1 btw)

items.title:
I'm using https://t.co/afnVLf3fJU in the video.

It's incredibly easy to create an agent that operat...
items.content:
I'm using https://t.co/afnVLf3fJU in the video.

It's incredibly easy to create an agent that operates a site. You can define the tasks you want to complete, and then access them through their API.

A huge benefit of this:

You'll communicate with the site back and forth using structured data. The whole process of running a computer, navigating the site, and figuring out what to do is all hidden behind the scenes.

By the way, this works even with sites protected with MFA.

Thanks to the @deck_agent team for giving me access to this and partnering with me on this post.
items.author: @svpino

items.content_html:
I'm using https://t.co/afnVLf3fJU in the video.

It's incredibly easy to create an agent that operates a site. You can define the tasks you want to complete, and then access them through their API.

A huge benefit of this:

You'll communicate with the site back and forth using structured data. The whole process of running a computer, navigating the site, and figuring out what to do is all hidden behind the scenes.

By the way, this works even with sites protected with MFA.

Thanks to the @deck_agent team for giving me access to this and partnering with me on this post.

items.title:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but beca...
items.content:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but because they are still thinking at the model layer.

The real shift is happening one level up, where AI becomes a system that can reason, use tools, retain context, and execute work inside secure environments.

That changes enterprise strategy faster than most leaders realize.

A thread, with insights from my latest video with NVIDIA…

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC

items.summary_html: Van Loon argues enterprise AI strategies are behind because they think at the model layer. Real shift is AI systems that reason, use tools, retain context, and execute in secure environments. NVIDIA partner content.
items.content_html:
Most enterprise AI strategies are already behind.

Not because they picked the wrong model, but because they are still thinking at the model layer.

The real shift is happening one level up, where AI becomes a system that can reason, use tools, retain context, and execute work inside secure environments.

That changes enterprise strategy faster than most leaders realize.

A thread, with insights from my latest video with NVIDIA…

#NVIDIAPartner #NVIDIAGTC

My takeaway:

Audio is finally becoming interactive.
You don’t mix sound anymore, you point at intent.

That’s what Meta’s SAM Audio project is pointing toward, and it has real implications for creators, product teams, and anyone building with multimodal AI.

items.title: 💾 I renamed it to https://t.co/6tRObkZbZa because AltaVista is still owned by Yahoo so I didn't wann...
items.content:
💾 I renamed it to https://t.co/6tRObkZbZa because AltaVista is still owned by Yahoo so I didn't wanna infringe

🔎 https://t.co/HmRnyRsZoI is a fully functional search engine and proxy that lets ANY old browser browse most of the web with just HTTP (instead of HTTPS), it converts modern web pages to something that any old device can access (like removing JavaScript code)

So now devices like your old Apple II, Commodore 64, Atari ST, Amiga, 486 PC, Dreamcast or PS2 can access the modern internet too! 🤓

And it's great for Kindles too because it converts most webpages to easy-to-read text ☺️

items.summary: Levelsio announces renaming his retro search engine/proxy project (avoiding AltaVista trademark). The tool converts modern HTTPS pages to HTTP for old browsers and devices like Apple II, Commodore 64, Kindle etc.

That is the real bottleneck, and it gets worse as companies move from one copilot to hundreds of autonomous agents.

Here’s the breakdown...

The winners in enterprise AI will not be the companies with access to the most models.

If you want the full breakdown, watch the video and tell me what part of your AI stack you are rethinking first.

items.title:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system ...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway:

Models are becoming components, not products.

What matters now is the system around them:

→ runtimes
→ memory
→ tool access
→ orchestration
→ secure execution environments

That is what turns a model into an agent, and an agent into something the business can actually deploy across functions.

items.title:
Under the hood, the model does one powerful thing:

It splits sound into two parts →

→ The target s...
items.content:
Under the hood, the model does one powerful thing:

It splits sound into two parts →

→ The target sound
→ Everything else

That single capability unlocks a lot →

→ Faster editing for creators
→ Cleaner, more controllable data for AI training
→ Multimodal systems that understand sound like visuals

This is basically segmentation, but for audio.

That single capability unlocks a lot →

→ Multimodal systems that understand sound like visuals

This is basically segmentation, but for audio.

items.title:
The big takeaway for me:

Before scaling AI, build the foundation.

→ Start with the business proble...
items.content:
The big takeaway for me:

Before scaling AI, build the foundation.

→ Start with the business problem, not the model
→ Define the process, policies, and metrics
→ Govern access to certified data products
→ Keep humans in the loop to validate and monitor

That is how you get better decisions at scale, not just faster mistakes.

Watch the full video for the full conversation with Collibra, then tell me this:

What is the biggest context gap holding back AI in your organization right now?

#CollibraPartner

items.title:
The clearest analogy is self-service BI.

We democratized data.
We sped up access.
But we also creat...
items.content:
The clearest analogy is self-service BI.

We democratized data.
We sped up access.
But we also created endless KPI debates.

Why?

→ Different teams used different definitions
→ Different reports showed different numbers
→ Humans could still stop, argue, and align

AI agents do not do that naturally.

They act on whatever context they are given, or not given.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon draws analogy between self-service BI problems (conflicting KPIs/definitions) and AI agent risks - agents act on whatever context given without stopping to argue.
items.reasoning: Interesting analogy between BI democratization challenges and AI agent risks. Part of sponsored thread but contains a useful insight.

items.title:
The second misconception I keep seeing:

Too many teams think they have to choose between open model...
items.content:
The second misconception I keep seeing:

Too many teams think they have to choose between open models and proprietary models.

They do not.

The smarter path is hybrid.

→ proprietary models for scale and broad capability
→ open models for flexibility and control
→ orchestration layers to route each task to the best-fit model in real time

That is how you balance performance, cost, and governance without locking yourself into one lane.

items.title:
The big unlock is simple but profound:

Instead of forcing humans to adapt to audio tools, the model...
items.content:
The big unlock is simple but profound:

Instead of forcing humans to adapt to audio tools, the model adapts to human intent.

You can guide it three ways →

→ Type what you want, like “keep only the voice”
→ Click where the sound happens in the video
→ Mark a moment in time and say, the sound I care about is here

No guesswork. No fragile presets.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

You can guide it three ways →

→ Type what you want, like “keep only the voice”

→ Click where the sound happens in the video

→ Mark a moment in time and say, the sound I care about is here

No guesswork. No fragile presets.

items.title:
That is why “context” is not a soft concept.

It is the operating model of the business, made usable...
items.content:
That is why “context” is not a soft concept.

It is the operating model of the business, made usable for AI.

In practice, that means:

→ What does this metric actually mean?
→ When should this rule apply?
→ Why does this policy exist?
→ What is this decision trying to optimize for?

If that is not explicit, AI starts guessing.

And guessed context is where enterprise AI breaks.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon argues that business 'context' is the operating model made usable for AI - missing context causes enterprise AI failure.
items.reasoning: Part of sponsored Collibra thread. Makes valid point about context being critical for enterprise AI but low engagement.

items.title: GPT with LangGraph! #BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorc...
items.content:
GPT with LangGraph! #BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

The rapid advancement of large model technology is leading to an increasing application of agent technology across various fields and industries significantly transforming how people work and live. In complex and dynamic environments, multi-agent systems are able to tackle intricate tasks that would be challenging for a single agent, thanks to their collaborative and division-of-labor approaches. 

The following stack of research papers and hands on tutorials highlight the integrated use of GPT with  LangGraph and CrewAI. LangGraph enhances information transmission efficiency through its graph-based structure, while CrewAI boosts team collaboration and system performance via intelligent task allocation and resource management. The key areas of this research include: 

The design of agent architectures based on LangGraph for precise control . The enhancement of agent capabilities through CrewAI to tackle a range of tasks. The goal of this study is to explore the combined potential of GPT and LangGraph and CrewAI in multi-agent systems, offering fresh insights for the ongoing evolution of agent technology and fostering innovation in the application of large model intelligent agents.

References 

Duan, Z., & Wang, J. (2024, November 27). Exploration of LLM multi-agent application implementation based on LangGraph+CrewAI. arXiv. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/pc2HNby2wx

Horsey, J. (2025, March 9). Build a powerful Python chatbot in minutes with LangGraph. Geeky Gadgets. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/xpp0gyYrgg

Ong, R. (2024, July 10). GPT-4o and LangGraph tutorial: Build a TNT-LLM application. DataCamp. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/wzv4BeoSlC

Sivan, V. (2024). Building AI agent systems with LangGraph. Medium. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/oJO20UXX4n

Wang, J., & Duan, Z. (2024, December 2). Intelligent Spark agents: A modular LangGraph framework for scalable, visualized, and enhanced big data machine learning workflows. arXiv. Retrieved March 9, 2025, from https://t.co/IONuxFVJZZ

items.title:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical compon...
items.content:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical component of effective healthcare. Obesity is a significant risk factor for a range of serious diseases, including chronic conditions, type-2 diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers. Understanding a person's obesity status can serve as a powerful catalyst for individuals to take control of their weight. Additionally, intentional weight management not only mitigates health risks but also offers the compelling benefit of reducing disease susceptibility.

Despite its widespread use, the Body Mass Index (BMI) – the standard metric defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) – fails to capture the complexities of obesity, as it overlooks essential body-type variations. Furthermore, nutritional needs differ markedly across regions and body types, underscoring the necessity for a more nuanced approach to obesity assessment.

Traditional anthropometric measurements, while effective, are often impractical due to the requirement for trained professionals to perform them accurately. In response to this challenge, innovative research utilizing 3D scanning technology is gaining momentum as a less-invasive and more accessible alternative. Unlike Computed Tomography (CT) or Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)—considered the gold standard for measuring body fat percentage (bf%)—3D scanners eliminate the risks associated with radiation exposure during frequent assessments.

Moreover, evaluating health risks demands a multifaceted approach rather than relying solely on a singular measure. In this study, we collected paired data from 3D body scans and DXA for a Korean population, providing a more comprehensive understanding of obesity and paving the way for improved health management strategies. This pioneering research has the potential to transform how we assess and respond to obesity, ultimately leading to healthier outcomes for individuals and communities alike.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode  

References

Jeon, S., Kim, M., Yoon, J., & et al. (2023). Machine learning-based obesity classification considering 3D body scanner measurements. Scientific Reports, 13, 3299. Retrieved February 27, 2025, from https://t.co/h3ZR1dBTMI

items.summary: GP Pulipaka writes about using ML with 3D body scanning for obesity classification as an alternative to BMI.

items.content:
MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning! @MIT

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month.

He gets discussed so often. It is a brilliant way to differentiate him from the others in the community (I have a list at https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ) and get everyone to talk. Which builds audience.

Is he lying? No. Just being a bit of a butthead in a strategic way.

items.title:
Xenobiotic Intelligence

Bio-Cybernetic Hex Swarm: Panspermia and the Alien Cephalopod Theory

The C...
items.content:
Xenobiotic Intelligence

Bio-Cybernetic Hex Swarm: Panspermia and the Alien Cephalopod Theory

The Cambrian Singularity: Extraterrestrial Life and the Origin of Octopuses

This research paper explores the Cambrian Singularity, addressing the classic paradox of whether the chicken or the egg came first. The research paper suggests that octopuses could be aliens from space and not originated from the earth. This goes simply against the Darwinian evolution theory and suggests that life always existed in the universe and nothing ever has evolved on the earth or other exoplanets .

The paper simply takes you back to the bible. These brainy cephalopods might have originated off the solar system. It supports the Cambrian explosion thesis which suggests that, all the organisms on the earth make up the most modern animal life as an outcome of extraterrestrial viruses carried on a meteor that crashed onto Earth 540 million years ago.

The underlying theory is panspermia, that some viruses and bacteria travel on the tails of comets or meteors that may take root on exoplanets. These comets could have introduced Earth to novel life forms that evolved on other exoplanets, including viruses, durable microorganisms like unearthly tardigrades or, as the new study suggests, even fertilized animal eggs from other worlds.

The paper suggests that cryopreserved eggs of either squids or octopuses could have arrived in icy bodies several hundred million years ago from the other parts of the universe that already had preexisting life. Crazy as this may sound, but the authors of this paper have presented a lot of interesting evidence for other scientists to mull over. Beginning with the studies of the nucleus of comet Halley in 1980s, comets were carrying bacteria and viruses from external space.

Their skill at things like opening jars is legendary. You cannot possibly find that intelligence from any other form of animal life in the sea on the earth.  Dawkins has always criticized creationism and appearance of some sort of intelligent design, but If life on Earth today is the result of countless tiny changes over billions of years, a clear chain of fossils leading back to that original single-celled organism should be available in the fossil record. There should be billions of fossils documenting the transitions between billions of species throughout the record. However, this prediction has not been verified in the fossil record, effectively falsifying Darwinian Evolution. Also no theory of evolution explained the sudden appearance of few species on the earth scene without any form of transition of intermediate forms which is the fundamental essence of evolution. This was acknowledged by the famous evolutionary paleontologist of Harvard, Stephen J. Gould.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode

References

Abbott, A. (2015). Octopus genome holds clues to uncanny intelligence. Nature. https://t.co/A413A7FMDU

Budd, G. E., & Jensen, S. (2017). The origin of animals and a ‘Savannah’ hypothesis for early animal evolution. Biological Reviews, 92(1), 446-473. https://t.co/zXDKgw7Lek

Hoyle, F., & Wickramasinghe, N. C. (2012). A short history of panspermia from antiquity through the mid-1970s. Astrobiology, 12(6), 471-476. https://t.co/lqmrsPkWXW

Paterson, J. R., Edgecombe, G. D., García-Bellido, D. C., Jago, J. B., & Gehling, J. G. (2013). Complexity and diversity of eyes in Early Cambrian ecosystems. Scientific Reports, 3, 2751. https://t.co/1ycpEYDPqU

Peters, S. E., & Gaines, R. R. (2018). Formation of the Great Unconformity as a trigger for the Cambrian explosion. Nature, 554(7690), 363-366. https://t.co/sBcuY3QSL0

items.summary_html: GP Pulipaka posts extensive writeup about panspermia theory, suggesting octopuses could be extraterrestrial in origin, challenging Darwinian evolution.
items.content_html:
Xenobiotic Intelligence

Bio-Cybernetic Hex Swarm: Panspermia and the Alien Cephalopod Theory

The Cambrian Singularity: Extraterrestrial Life and the Origin of Octopuses

This research paper explores the Cambrian Singularity, addressing the classic paradox of whether the chicken or the egg came first. The research paper suggests that octopuses could be aliens from space and not originated from the earth. This goes simply against the Darwinian evolution theory and suggests that life always existed in the universe and nothing ever has evolved on the earth or other exoplanets .

The paper simply takes you back to the bible. These brainy cephalopods might have originated off the solar system. It supports the Cambrian explosion thesis which suggests that, all the organisms on the earth make up the most modern animal life as an outcome of extraterrestrial viruses carried on a meteor that crashed onto Earth 540 million years ago.

The underlying theory is panspermia, that some viruses and bacteria travel on the tails of comets or meteors that may take root on exoplanets. These comets could have introduced Earth to novel life forms that evolved on other exoplanets, including viruses, durable microorganisms like unearthly tardigrades or, as the new study suggests, even fertilized animal eggs from other worlds.

The paper suggests that cryopreserved eggs of either squids or octopuses could have arrived in icy bodies several hundred million years ago from the other parts of the universe that already had preexisting life. Crazy as this may sound, but the authors of this paper have presented a lot of interesting evidence for other scientists to mull over. Beginning with the studies of the nucleus of comet Halley in 1980s, comets were carrying bacteria and viruses from external space.

Their skill at things like opening jars is legendary. You cannot possibly find that intelligence from any other form of animal life in the sea on the earth. Dawkins has always criticized creationism and appearance of some sort of intelligent design, but If life on Earth today is the result of countless tiny changes over billions of years, a clear chain of fossils leading back to that original single-celled organism should be available in the fossil record. There should be billions of fossils documenting the transitions between billions of species throughout the record. However, this prediction has not been verified in the fossil record, effectively falsifying Darwinian Evolution. Also no theory of evolution explained the sudden appearance of few species on the earth scene without any form of transition of intermediate forms which is the fundamental essence of evolution. This was acknowledged by the famous evolutionary paleontologist of Harvard, Stephen J. Gould.

I also know that if you didn't know about Interstate 60 before I told you, you also don't know about Knockin' on Heaven's Door (1997).

It's another movie you wish you had watched sooner and that will mark you for the rest of your life.

Enjoy.
